data.title: Health and Happiness: The Good And The Bad
data.description:
My rating for Health and Happiness (H&H) International Holdings Limited (OTCPK:BTSDF) is a Hold. The good thing regarding H&H is the positive growth outlook for its supplements business. The bad thing about BTSDF is its infant milk formula business' unfavorable prospects. The stock is now trading at a Price-to-Earnings Growth or PEG multiple of about 1x, and this means that the company's expected earnings growth rate is factored into its earnings multiple. As such, I maintain a Hold rating for H&H.

H&H describes itself as a "health and nutrition company for the whole family" on the company's website. BTSDF generated 66% and 27% of its 1H 2024 top line from nutritional supplements and infant milk formulas, respectively. The Mainland Chinese market contributed over two-thirds or 68% of H&H's 2024 interim revenue. These figures are taken from the company's 2024 interim report. My focus is on H&H's nutritional supplements and infant milk formula businesses in China for the current article, taking into consideration the company's revenue mix.

BTSDF's 2020 interim results were the subject of my earlier September 29, 2020 write-up.

Investors should be aware that they can deal in H&H's shares on the Over-The-Counter market and the Stock Exchange of Hong Kong. The trading liquidity for the company's OTC shares is poor. But readers can trade in H&H's relatively more liquid Hong Kong-listed shares with US brokerages like Interactive Brokers or Hong Kong securities firms such as Boom Securities. As a reference, the three-month average daily trading value for the company's Hong Kong-listed shares was roughly $500,000 based on S&P Capital IQ data.

Supplements Business Is A Leading Player In A Growing Market

H&H reports its full financial results on a semi-annual basis as a Hong Kong-listed company. The company's recent 1H 2024 top line performance was mixed with the strength of the supplements business offsetting the weakness of the infant milk formula business. On a constant currency basis, BTSDF's VHMS (Vitamins, Herbs, Minerals & Supplements) product sales grew by +13.0% YoY in the 2024 interim period, while its infant milk formula product revenue decreased by -18.8% YoY during the same time frame.

I will write about H&H's supplements and infant milk formula businesses in this section and the next section, respectively.

There are good reasons to believe that the positive revenue growth momentum for BTSDF's supplements business can be sustained in the foreseeable future. The supplements market in China has significant growth potential, and H&H is a market leader in this space.

The value of "China's health supplements market" has doubled from RMB 161.4 billion in 2017 to RMB328.2 billion in 2023, according to a July 16, 2024, article posted on China Briefing, a China-focused media publication established by consulting firm Dezan Shira & Associates.

It is realistic to think that the Chinese supplements industry has a long growth runway.

As per Chinese consumer research firm CBNData's January 2024 write-up (translated using Google), the average amount of money that a consumer spends on supplements in China on a yearly basis is less than a fifth of that for other countries like Germany and the US.

Separately, Chinese online retail giant JD.com, Inc. (JD) disclosed on its blog that "sales of probiotics and health tonics increased more than tenfold and threefold, respectively" on the company's platform at this year's China 618 e-commerce festival. China's per-capita spending on supplements is expected to increase going forward, as consumers in the country become more health-conscious as evidenced by the recent health product sales data shared by JD.

H&H's supplements business should be able to capitalize on the long-term growth of China's supplements industry with its leading market position.

In its investor presentation slides, BTSDF highlighted that its Swisse brand is the second-largest player in China's overall VHMS sector. The company's Swisse brand is ranked first in the online segment of the Chinese VHMS segment and certain supplement categories like "beauty, women's health, men's health, detox, heart health, joint health" in China as per its investor presentation disclosures. In the July 16, 2024, China Briefing article referred to above, it was indicated that the Swiss brand boasted a 29% market share in the "vitamins and minerals" segment of China's supplements market.

Safety is definitely a key consideration for consumers selecting supplement brands, and trusted brands are often the leading brands that are known to most people. This means that top brands like Swisse will most likely retain their market leadership in the absence of any negative events affecting their brand equity and reputation.

In summary, the Chinese supplements market has good growth prospects, and BTSDF's Swisse brand is one of the top players in the China market. This bodes well for H&H's VHMS sales outlook.

But Infant Milk Formula Business Faces Headwinds

BTSDF's infant milk formula business segment operating under the Biostime brand has to deal with challenges like a decreasing birth rate and stiff competition in China.

A declining birth rate for the Chinese market translates into a smaller addressable market for H&H's infant milk formula products.

China's birth rate measured in "births per 1,000 inhabitants" was 6.39 last year according to data sourced from Statista. As a comparison, the country's annual birth rate was consistently above 12 between 2000 and 2017. Reuters recently published an article on October 18, 2024, mentioning that "China's National Health Commission" has initiated a survey to "understand factors influencing" the "fear surrounding having children." The low birth rate in China is a serious issue, and it isn't entirely certain what has deterred many Chinese from having more babies which has justified a new survey from the Chinese government. This implies that China's birth rate could stay low for the foreseeable future.

The Chinese dairy sector has taken a hit from a declining number of new births in China. A September 24, 2024, Newsweek report noted that there is "an oversupply of milk amid a flagging birth rate" and highlighted the "significantly reduced demand for infant formula" in the country. A good proxy for the health of China's infant milk formula industry is China Feihe Limited's (OTCPK:CFEIY) (OTCPK:CHFLF) [6186:HK] top line performance. China Feihe is the biggest infant milk formula company in China as per Frost & Sullivan research as indicated on its website. China Feihe's revenue in local currency or RMB terms contracted by -6.4% and -8.3% for FY 2022 and FY 2023, respectively as per S&P Capital IQ data.

On the other hand, H&H has ceded ground to its rivals in a shrinking Chinese infant milk formula market.

It is worth noting that China Feihe's top line performance improved in 1H 2024 with a +3.7% YoY revenue growth. In contrast, H&H's infant milk formula sales for the China market decreased by -19.0% YoY during the same time period. In the company's investor presentation slides, H&H cited "increased competitive intensity" as a headwind for its infant milk formula business, and it revealed that its market share in the overall China infant milk formula decreased by -0.6 percentage points YoY to 4.7% in 1H 2024.

H&H is ranked seventh with a 4.7% market share in terms of total infant milk formula sales for the Chinese market. But BTSDF is only the 11th largest player with regard to infant milk formula sales for the e-commerce sales channel in the country boasting a relatively lower 3.1% market share. It is reasonable to think that H&H has a comparatively weaker competitive position in the e-commerce sales channel, which could have hurt its overall infant milk formula sales in China.

In a nutshell, H&H is facing substantial headwinds like tougher competition and a decreasing number of childbirths in China.

Final Thoughts

H&H's overall top line decreased by -4.1% YoY in 1H 2024, which was far worse than the company's 1H 2023 revenue expansion of +17.2% YoY. On a full-year basis, BTSDF's revenue growth is projected to slow significantly from +9.0% last year to +0.3% this year as per S&P Capital IQ data. While the long-term growth outlook for H&H's supplements business is favorable, the company's short-term top line performance has been negatively impacted by the weakness of its infant milk formula business. It is fair to say there are both good and bad businesses within the company.

BTSDF's mixed prospects relating to both its supplements and infant milk formula businesses are appropriately reflected in the stock's valuations. H&H is currently trading at a PEG (Price-to-Earnings Growth) or PEG multiple of 1.1 times, which suggests that the stock is fairly valued. As per S&P Capital IQ data, the company's consensus FY 2023-2026 normalized EPS CAGR is an unexciting +8%, while the stock trades at a modest 9 times consensus next twelve months' normalized P/E.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: FXI: I Expect The Chinese Euphoria To Pass (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Main Thesis/Background

The purpose of this article is to discuss the iShares China Large-Cap ETF (NYSEARCA:FXI) as an investment option at its current market price. This is a reasonable option for domestic investors who want exposure to China as the fund's objective is to "track the investment results of an index composed of large-capitalization Chinese equities that trade on the Hong Kong Exchange".

It has been a while since I covered FXI - or Chinese equities more broadly - but readers would remember I have been a bull in the past. In fact, since my last positive review, FXI has indeed posted a positive return. However, this pales in comparison to what US stock markets have delivered in the interim:

Given this reality, I thought now was a decent time to take another look at FXI. After all, since the Chinese government announced stimulus efforts to revive their economy, FXI has been on a tear:

This momentum has certainly been making plenty of noise, but the fact is it has stalled a bit in the immediate term. For me, I think the gains in Chinese equities, and FXI by extension, have come in a bit too far, too fast. This leads me to a more cautious stance going forward, which I will outline below. The net result is I am shifting from a "buy" to a "hold" rating, as I feel it would be prudent to wait for a better opportunity in the future.

It Seems Like Everyone Has Rushed In

The very first point I want to touch on is that I think this play has gotten overblown on a broad scale. What I mean by this is that it seems like everyone and their mother have been rushing into Chinese equities. This has come at the expense of other Asian geographies, as investors have believed (or perhaps hoped) that the Chinese economy is going to turn more positive in the months and years ahead. In the short term, we have witnessed a massive inflow of fresh capital into Chinese funds, while other areas have stalled:

This is not a "bad" thing for Chinese equities. In fact, it is quite good. It helps restore confidence and suggests that the earlier selling has tapped out. But my followers know I like to position myself as more of a contrarian investor. So buying into - or recommending - ideas after the market has shifted this dramatically is not something I have a habit of doing.

This is a fundamental point. Whether or not the Chinese economy is about to turn the page is a projection. But the equity market has priced in that it will because investors saw others rushing in and "fear of missing out" took over a bit, in my view. So what we are left with now is equity prices that are elevated from earlier this year, but China still faces many of the same headwinds. In this type of environment, I see a lot of merit to being cautious. That is central to why I believe a downgrade to "hold" is the right call.

Why Not Be A Bear? Equities Are Relatively Cheap

I definitely have concerns about the broader economic climate in China, and also the fact that I personally feel equities there have gotten a bit ahead of themselves. I stand by that call going forward. But readers will note I placed "hold" on FXI, not "sell". This isn't to play both sides of the fence, but rather to exemplify that there are legitimate reasons for being optimistic on Chinese equities. This push-pull reality results in a backdrop where I would find it a bit too risky to be too bearish on this thematic idea.

To justify this, consider that Chinese equities had been underperforming the world until very recently. This has left them trading at valuations that are not only relatively cheap to their peers but also with regard to their own trading history:

For this simple reason, I think the downside potential is a bit limited right now. Yes, Chinese stocks could give up some of their recent gains. That is a big reason why I am not a buyer here. But I also see a bottom to how low they can go because valuations are historically cheap. Until this changes, it will be hard for me to suggest being a straight-line seller.

Distribution Signals Weakness

I will now shift the conversation to FXI specifically. This is relevant because I have a "hold" rating on this fund in isolation, and there are numerous ways to play this space. FXI holds predominately large-cap companies in China, which theoretically should be lower risk than smaller businesses.

However, I have concerns with this thesis because of what the distribution is telling us. FXI saw a dramatic drop in its payout in the first half of the year compared to 2023, which should raise some eyebrows:

June 2023 Distribution June 2024 Distribution YOY Change
$.154/share $.085/share (45%)

Source: iShares

This signals the underlying firms within FXI's portfolio have either cash-flow problems or management is concerned about future growth/profitability. Neither of these scenarios inspires a lot of confidence in me.

Of course, these companies could be curtailing dividend payouts in order to invest for future growth. That would be a worthwhile endeavor and a reasonable trade-off. But I don't think that is the case here. When it happens in isolation or across a particular sector, I could buy that story. But even as a single-country ETF, FXI is still a diversified fund and this speaks to a broader message of weakness in the underlying Chinese economy rather than prudent fiscal discipline on the part of corporate management. This is another supporting factor for downgrading my rating right now.

This Fund Is Heavily Dependent On The Consumer

The next topic for discussion is the make-up of FXI's holdings. Specifically, I want to explain that the fund's heavy tilt toward the Consumer Discretionary sector is a source of concern for me. This is no small attribute for this fund, with this one sector alone representing over 1/3 of total assets:

For me, the forward outlook for retail and discretionary spend within China is very mixed. Yes, the government has announced some stimulus measures and brighter days could be ahead. The message from the central government only goes so far to actual spending. To predict how loose consumers will be with their wallets in the future, we need to consider consumer/household sentiment. On this metric alone, things look quite scary:

It is hard to sugarcoat this metric. To be fair, the contrarian bell could be ringing for some - and I wouldn't necessarily fault them. But we should recognize that consumer sentiment is a broad measure of how the Chinese feel about their current and future prospects, and it isn't going to rapidly change overnight. This is going to take time and, even if it does improve, the current level is so depressed that a moderate rise in this metric won't do much to improve my own confidence about Chinese equities going forward.

Stimulus Announcements Need To Have Teeth

My final point touches on the upcoming change in Chinese fiscal and monetary policy going forward. As the market has reacted positively to news that the current government is going to take steps to give the domestic economy a boost. Among the steps the government is considering are detailed below:

All good news for investors, right? In fairness, it may be. But I would interject with a tone of caution here for two reasons.

One, the market has already rallied on the news. The best time to buy would have been before this announcement. Buying in now is a bit late to the game in the sense that I would want to see actual results, not just policy actions. Can equities rally still? They could, but I see the recent pullback off the highs for funds like FXI as a sign the market's euphoria is cooling a bit.

Two, what the Chinese government wants to do and what it actually does can be two different things. And this isn't meant to be an insult or a slight, just an observation of how previous announcements have not always materialized.

For example, if we look at planned spending for 2024 (before the recent announcement) we see that local government spending has actually trailed anticipated spending for the year. In fact, this lag was pretty consistent and meaningful across the board:

What I take away from this is that I need to see action more than words when it comes to my investment strategy within China. When I hear plans for the future, I perk up, but I will not take plans to the bank. I will wait for some signs of actual change and/or results before committing my hard-earned dollars toward new policy goals in China. This is especially true for funds like FXI that are singularly centered on this country. For me, this is confirmation that a downgrade to "hold" is the right move.

Bottom Line

Chinese stocks got a big boost of late and that was certainly a welcome move for the bulls. While we could see brighter days ahead due to increased government spending, fewer regulations, and lower interest rates, I would caution against getting too carried away. The market has already rewarded equities with a big push higher, and that tells me further gains will be more difficult to come by. Investors will need to see improved profit figures and not just slogans from the government. That is going to take time.

Further, consumer sentiment is extremely negative right now, and that means spending is not likely to impress in the months ahead. For funds like FXI that rely heavily on the consumer discretionary sector, this poses a headwind. As a result, I believe a rating shift to a more neutral stance is appropriate, and I would suggest to my followers they approach any new positions very selectively at this time.

data.title: Arcosa: Still Not Cheap Enough
data.description:
The Thesis

Arcosa (NYSE: NYSE:ACA) continued its topline growth after a weak 2023, as it exited the first half of the year due to steady growth in the construction product segment and benefit from contribution from the recent acquisition of Ameron in the engineered structures segment.

I expect this growth to continue further in the rest of 2024 as demand remains healthy across most of the business with healthy order activity, which along with benefits from backlog and continued contribution from acquisition should help in sustaining topline growth in the quarters ahead. Margins should also remain good with pricing strength and benefits from operational improvement. Since my last article on ACA, the stock has been up in the low double-digit underperforming the market. Currently, the stock is trading at a premium to both its historical average and sector median.

While the company's long-term appears to be promising primarily due to the new Stavola acquisition, its forward growth rate with mid-single-digit EPS growth does not justify this premium valuation to me. Therefore, I would like to stay on the sidelines for now, as I expect this underperformance to continue further.

Last Quarter Performance

The company’s top line was up in the low single digits in 2023, followed by lower revenue in the Engineered segment due to the divestiture of its storage tank business. However, the first half has been good so far, with its revenue increasing by 13.7% to $664.7 million, as it exited the first half of 2024. This double-digit growth resulted from a strong 33% year-on-year increase in Engineered Structures segment revenue, which primarily benefited from higher volume in utility structure and contribution from a recent acquisition, Ameron. The Construction Product segment on the other hand was up mid-single digits due to the benefit of strong pricing in its aggregated business, more than offsetting the impact from volume decline. The Transportation Products segment was nearly flat with a 0.7% year-on-year increase in the second quarter of 2024.

Higher revenue across all the segments also benefitted the company’s margin during the quarter as its adjusted EBITDA jumped 240 bps to 17%. This was driven by strong margins in the construction segment, which saw an increase of nearly 360 bps, versus the prior year's same quarter due to benefits from strong pricing and operational improvements in certain parts of this business. EBITDA growth also benefited the company's bottom line growth as its adjusted EPS increased to $0.91 from $0.76 a year ago, beating the consensus estimates by $0.08 in the second quarter of 2024.

Outlook

As the company exited the first half of the year, its topline continued its steady growth after a slowdown in 2023, as the company benefited from significant contributions from recent acquisitions and favorable pricing across some parts of the business. I expect this growth to continue further in the latter half of the year, as the demand environment remains healthy, primarily in the utility structure and wind tower business of the Engineered structures segment, which along with a strong $1.3 billion backlog related to wind and utility structure should drive topline growth in the quarters ahead. In addition to this, the strong pricing momentum also continued across the company’s construction product segment, which along with expected volume improvement in the coming quarters, after the recent decline due to elevated rainfall in the region, should benefit the company’s revenue in the quarter ahead.

Talking about the Transportation product segment, positive momentum continued in liquid barges order booking as the company exited the first half of the year. In the last quarter itself, total barge orders were approximately $33 million, which largely includes tank barges. The barge-related backlog also remains elevated at nearly $252 million as of the end of the second quarter, with 70% expected to be converted in the remainder of 2024. In my view, this should further support the company’s consolidated revenue growth in 2024.

While the near-term prospects look favorable for the company, contributions from new upcoming acquisitions and benefits from project completions should drive topline growth beyond 2024. In 2023, the company announced to acquire Stavola, a leading provider of construction materials, for $1.2 billion. This is the largest acquisition in the company’s history that is expected to enhance the company’s portfolio significantly and expand its market presence across the largest MSA in the U.S., which should further strengthen the company's market position in the region. In addition to this, the company has also invested significantly in certain projects in the past two years to drive long-term organic growth, which is expected to be completed by year-end 2024.

As strategic acquisitions remain a key part of the company’s long-term growth, it continued to focus on potential M&As that strategically fit and help the company enhance its offerings and expand its geographic footprint. Currently, the company’s leverage ratio stands at 3.7 times, which is notably higher than its targeted ratio of 2 to 2.5 times, primarily due to the financing for the Stavola acquisition. However, the company remains focused on reducing debt, using proceeds from the sale of some non-core and loss-making businesses such as the steel components business and subscale asphalt business. In my view, this should result in improved leverage in the coming years, further supporting future acquisitions and benefiting the company’s topline growth in the long term.

Valuation

I last covered ACA earlier this year in February and gave it a hold rating due to its premium valuation as compared to its historical average and sector median. Since then, the stock has been underperforming and is currently up nearly 11% against a 17% increase in the S&P 500. Currently, the company's stock is trading at a forward non-GAAP P/E ratio of 27.15, based on the FY24 EPS of $3.54, representing a mid-single-digit growth year on year. In comparison with its five-year average of 24.64, the stock looks slightly overvalued. In comparison with its sector median of 20.81x on the other hand, the stock is trading at approximately a 31% premium.

Company Forward P/E (non-GAAP) Forward Revenue growth Forward EBITDA growth
(ACA) Arcosa 27.15x 9.61% 18.71%
(STRL) Sterling Infrastructure 29.01x 9.60% 16.95%
(ROAD) Construction Partners 59.23x 16.92% 32.81%
(PRIM) Primoris Services Corporation 18.97x 13.84% 17.38%
(DY) Dycom Industries 25.63x 11.27% 21.95%

As we can see in the table above, while the company is expected to continue its growth across both the top line and margins, it does not provide the best value against its closest peers, which are also expected to grow at a similar or better forward growth rate with an even better multiple.

I expect the company's revenue to benefit from healthy demand, favorable pricing, and contribution from the recent acquisition, which along with a focus on operation improvement should benefit the margins in the longer term. In addition to this, focusing on reducing debt through the proceeds of the sale of non-core businesses and contribution from the new higher margin Stavola acquisition should help the bottom-line growth in the coming year, which could improve valuation in the future. However, considering the current multiple and single-digit EPS growth rate for FY24, I consider the stock valuation too expensive at the moment.

Conclusion

As we discussed earlier, the company's stock is trading above both its sector median and historical average. The near term looks favorable as demand remains good in certain parts of the business which along with support from recent acquisitions, the company should continue its top-line growth in 2024. Strong pricing on the other hand should drive margins in the quarters ahead. While the long-term growth prospects remain good, I would suggest a 'Hold' rating on this stock at the current levels due to its premium valuation.

Please see the important performance and other related disclosures at the end of this Commentary, which are an integral part of this quarterly Commentary Newsletter.

The third quarter was even-handed for global markets, with most equity or credit markets posting mid-to-high single digit returns. Measures of economic activity were stable, and energy prices receded, although heightened tensions in the Middle East began reversing that recently. Most central banks either began (U.S., U.K., Switzerland) or continued (EU) cutting their policy rates, with the notable exception of Japan-though rates there remain a rounding error away from zero.

Within equities, developed markets showered greater rewards on smaller or value-oriented stocks, while the reverse was true in emerging markets. After a weak first half in 2024, beta was one of the strongest factors in global equities this quarter.

As our investment team meets with companies, reviews recent earnings reports, and surveys the global landscape, they note several investment dynamics that inform our positioning:

>As the U.S. Federal Reserve began to ease rates, companies gained greater certainty about the future costs of capital.

  • That seems particularly beneficial for Information Technology, as many corporations begin to set their IT budgets for 2025. AI remains a dominant trend with many companies seeing greater risks of underspending and being left behind rivals.
  • We do not see that happening widely yet for Industrials, where many companies also want to see the results of the presidential election before planning increased spending on capacity or automation. For instance, short-cycle industrial markets showed recent deterioration while longer-cycle markets linked to public infrastructure spending or various secular trends remained strong.

>In Japan, perhaps the most crucial short- to mid-term factors to consider is the interest rate set by Bank of Japan (BOJ) and its impact on the Yen:

  • While the BOJ is not ignoring imported inflation (energy, food, raw materials, etc.), they view it as highly transitory. Domestic inflation (wages) is seen as more permanent.
  • The recent rate hike led to a Yen appreciation, which allows the BOJ to assess how consumers are doing post recent wage increases.

> In Europe, the start of wage inflation and the ongoing reshoring/shift away from China and Russia are creating additional inflationary pressure.

  • Within IT Services, we saw profit warnings throughout the second and third quarters. Many large projects were delayed, leading to cost-cutting measures.
  • Among Industrials, strength persisted in longer-cycle end markets such as electrification, aerospace and defense, and infrastructure.

> The investment exodus from China continues, driven by economic or political considerations. We witness:

  • Companies and investors shift more of their focus to other Asian markets with better forecasted growth or stability.
  • Corporations move more production capacity closer to home.
  • China's domestic stimulus plans aim to counteract some of that, though it is focused on internal activities.

> In a market with slower growth and fewer severe macroeconomic or thematic disruptions, companies with strong internal structures that can generate growth independently of GDP are more likely to be rewarded.

The Global Small Cap Portfolio underperformed the MSCI World Small Cap benchmark during the third quarter of 2024.

Regional Performance: The Americas

Negative contribution from the region was mostly driven by the underweight position, particularly in the United States, and stock selections in Canada.

Among the wide variety of Information Technology companies, we prefer critical system providers, specialized component designers, systems that improve productivity or efficiency for their clients, and others that closely tie to increasing shares of corporate IT budgets. JFrog (FROG), a provider of systems to manage and speed the release of software updates from developers to users, reported results in line with earlier projections but reduced its guidance for the remainder of 2024. The caution arose from longer sales cycles among clients, who grew more concerned about the macroeconomic environment. After speaking with the company's management, we were encouraged by the underlying business strength. While there were delays in signing new contracts, those were not cancellations. Nevertheless, JFrog's shares retreated by -23%. Synaptics (SYNA), a developer of human interface semiconductor solutions for electronic devices, experienced a -12% decline in its share price. Despite reporting better-than-feared quarterly results, with revenues slightly above the consensus and earnings significantly exceeding expectations due to good cost control. Management indicated that the lingering inventory digestion is largely over. However, the end market recovery has been slower than anticipated.

Our preferences in the Consumer-oriented sectors lean toward value-oriented or specialty retailers, franchise models, premium brands, or support services for other consumer companies. Detracting from the results was Topgolf Callaway Brands (MODG), which manufactures golf equipment and accessories, as well as operates entertainment venues. Although recent channel checks indicated that TopGolf activity trends were improving, that softened later in the quarter. Overall revenues and earnings were less than anticipated, which led management to lower its guidance for the rest of the year. The company's Callaway equipment and apparel business was more stable, though management also discussed a strategic review of the entertainment business. Subsequently, the company announced the planned spinoff of TopGolf in 2025. While we agreed with the rationale, the timing was at a point of weakness, and we exited the position that was down -31% during the quarter. Performance Food Group (PFGC), a food products distributor operating through Foodservice and Vistar segments, reported results that exceeded market expectations, easing investor concerns about slowing restaurant traffic and weak convenience store sales. For fiscal year 2025, management provided guidance for in-line sales figures but above-expectations EBITDA, reflecting confidence in improved gross profit across segments. These positive developments led to a 19% increase in PFGC's share price.

Many of our Industrial positions provide necessary business-to-business operational services, highly technical components, automation & efficiency improvements, or essential infrastructure services. EMCOR Group (EME), a provider of electrical, mechanical, construction, and facilities services, saw its shares rise by 18%. The combination of strong results and a substantial new business backlog reinforced our positive outlook for EMCOR, leading us to increase our position. Regal Rexnord (RRX), specializing in motion control systems, climate solutions, and mechanical components, experienced a 23% increase in its share price despite mixed earnings. While the second quarter was strong, management lowered guidance to account for a softer second-half ramp in the Automation & Motion Control segment. The company's prospects in aerospace, data center, and medical markets remain solid. Canada-based Pason Systems (OTCQX:PSYTF), a designer and producer of sensors, instrumentation and data management systems used for drilling land rigs for oil and gas production, saw its share price decline by -26% following an inline second quarter. We anticipate the North American land drilling will remain near current levels in the remainder of 2024 before increasing in 2025 and beyond, with completions activity following a similar trajectory.

National Storage Affiliates Trust (NSA), a real estate investment trust focused on self-storage properties, benefited from the Federal Reserve's decision to lower interest rates. While new consumer demand remains soft, fundamentals started to improve with the limited new construction of self-storage facilities in the past two years and the absorption of excess supply. NSA's share price increased by 18%.

Regional Performance: Europe

Europe was the portfolio's largest regional contributor. There was relative strength in France and the United Kingdom and relative weakness in Denmark and Italy.

In Italy, shares of Amplifon (OTCPK:AMFPF), a leading global hearing aid distributor, pulled back -19%. They reported a slower-than-expected recovery in the European market and accelerated conversion of U.S. franchisees. Management also highlighted expansion opportunities in the U.S. and China. Denmark-based Ringkjobing Landbobank offers both traditional banking services and financial advisory services. The company is among the most efficient banks in the world based on its cost/income ratio. Concerns arose about the negative impact on net income from declining interest rates, along with management revising down their original FY2024 soft guidance during the year. The share price of Ringkjobing declined -6% and we added to our position on this price weakness. In the United Kingdom, wealth manager St. James's Place (OTCPK:STJPF) announced a strong beat to consensus estimates driven by robust inflows and a subsequent share buyback. The company also implemented a new cost-cutting program to align with the changing regulatory environment and its cost base. With the regulatory hurdle now cleared, shares of St. James's Place surged 40% during the quarter.

Regional Performance: Japan

Japan was another area of strength.

Rakuten Bank, Japan's leading digital bank, once again reported impressive results, with significant account gains. Although our investment thesis for Rakuten Bank does not include a rate hike by the Bank of Japan, higher interest rates have positively impacted the bank. Rakuten Bank's stock rose by 24%. Discount supermarket Kobe Bussan (OTCPK:KOBNF) reported a minor miss in its Q3 results but continued to see strong demand. Logistics costs are also improving due to increased efficiency. We trimmed our holdings as the stock rose 41%. We also reduced our position in Simplex, a leading systems developer and business consultant. The company reported mixed quarterly results, characterized by solid sales growth and a healthy backlog but a slower-than-expected start to earnings. Shares of Simplex declined by -4%.

During the quarter, we participated in the IPO of Timee, a Japanese job platform specializing in short-term job postings. Timee offers a unique and flexible solution for both jobseekers and employers. The company's core operations center around its digital platform, which connects jobseekers with employers seeking short-term and gig workers. With an average work length of four hours, Timee has established itself as a clear market leader by providing streamlined services that traditional HR companies have struggled to match efficiently.

Regional Performance: Asia Pacific ex Japan and Emerging Markets

Asia Pacific ex Japan was the portfolio's largest regional detractor due to the underweight position and stock selections. Australasia's largest insurance broker network, Steadfast Group (OTC:SFGLF), was negatively affected by an Australian news report involving Strata management companies, which included Steadfast as an extension. The report highlighted concerns about Strata operators, who are Steadfast's customers, not being transparent with underlying apartment owners about their dealing with insurance brokers. Due to regulatory uncertainty regarding the future of Strata management companies, we reduced our position in Steadfast, with a -4% decline in share price.

Though Emerging Markets lagged the portfolio's overall performance, the region holds one of the top contributors. Embraer (ERJ), Brazil's leading global aircraft manufacturing company, delivered strong performance. Operating in commercial aviation, executive jets, and defense and security, ERJ's second-quarter results exceeded consensus estimates on both revenue and earnings. Weakness at Boeing and subsequent announcement of Virgin Australia's purchase of E190-2 jets contributed to the strong momentum, propelling ERJ's shares up by 40% during the quarter. Given industry-wide challenges in aircraft deliveries and ERJ's already record-high backlog, we trimmed our position amidst this price strength.

Conclusion

Heading into the last quarter of 2024, with most central banks easing the lending rates the next questions are "how far?" and "how fast?". Equally, in front of mind is the outcome of November's U.S. presidential election, which will reverberate across domestic and non-U.S. economies. With that dynamic economic backdrop, our focus remains on projecting growth potential for our investments relative to their valuations and finding where those meaningfully differ from market expectations. As always, we are available for any questions you might have.

data.title: Gevo Makes Funding Progress But Competitive Risks Loom
data.description:
As a growth investor, I love investing in young companies with disruptive potential. However, this investment strategy exposes my portfolio to a significant risk of capital loss. To mitigate the risks associated with investing in unproven businesses, I try to maintain a diversified portfolio.

Gevo, Inc. (NASDAQ:GEVO) caught my attention last week when the company received a conditional commitment for $1.46 billion in funding from the U.S. Department of Energy Loan Programs Office. GEVO stock gained almost 50% following this announcement as investors cheered for the expected success of Gevo's Net-Zero 1 project in South Dakota. For those unfamiliar with the company, Gevo is a new energy company focused on the production of sustainable, low-carbon fuels and chemicals including sustainable aviation fuel, gasoline components, diesel fuel, and energy-dense liquid hydrocarbons.

The conditional funding for $1.46 billion will be a major boost for Gevo given that the company only had $245 million in cash and cash equivalents at the end of the last quarter. With the world increasingly moving towards a sustainable future, I wanted to dive deeper into Gevo's business to determine whether the company is investable today.

DOE's Conditional Funding Commitment For Gevo

Shares of Gevo jumped following the company's announcement that it received a conditional commitment for a loan guarantee with disbursements worth $1.46 billion from the U.S. Energy Department's Loan Programs Office. With capitalized interest during construction, the loan facility will have a borrowing capacity of $1.63 billion. This credit facility will help finance a jet fuel production facility using locally sourced feedstocks in Lake Preston, South Dakota, marking the company's first integrated, commercial-scale facility in the U.S. to convert cornstarch to sustainable aviation fuel with carbon capture and renewable power.

Gevo said the loan is for its Net-Zero 1 project planned in Lake Preston, South Dakota. The Net-Zero 1 project aims to boost the production of sustainable aviation fuel, renewable diesel, and renewable naphtha. With the completion of the Lake Preston facility, it will annually produce about 60 million gallons of SAF, along with around 1.3 billion pounds of protein and animal feed products and 30 million pounds of corn oil as co-products. NZ1 is the first-ever large-scale alcohol-to-jet project that produces low-carbon high-protein wet animal feed, net-zero fuel, and other co-products to receive such funding commitments from the DOE.

Exhibit 1: Gevo's SAF production flow

Commenting on the new loan facility, Gevo CEO Dr. Patrick Gruber said:

This marks a watershed moment for the Net-Zero 1 project and a critical step forward in Gevo's mission to transform the aviation industry by providing a scalable, sustainable, and economical renewable-carbon-based jet fuel-SAF.

The Department of Energy also approved another similar conditional loan guarantee commitment of up to $1.44 billion to Calumet Inc. (CLMT) to support the expansion of its facility in Montana. Upon completion, the facility will produce 315 million gallons of biofuels per year, mostly SAF, along with renewable diesel and renewable naphtha, using vegetable oils and grease.

Gevo asserts that its proprietary technology will produce SAF with a net-zero carbon footprint throughout its lifecycle, positioning the company at the forefront of sustainable fuel development. While the DOE's conditional commitment has buoyed investor sentiment toward the company, it is not a final financing agreement, since both parties must satisfy several conditions for the loan guarantee to be finalized.

The Promising Macroeconomic Picture

The conditional commitment announcements from the Department of Energy's LPO are part of the Biden-Harris administration's Investing in America agenda, which strives to strengthen local economies. The administration also aims to increase U.S. annual production of SAF to 3 billion gallons by 2030 and 35 billion gallons by 2050. This new commitment is also in line with the aviation sector's aim to meet decarbonization goals, where SAF is expected to play a key role.

The U.S. Energy Information Administration predicts domestic production of biofuels to increase by about 50% in 2024, led by rising SAF production. Currently, the airline industry is responsible for 11% of U.S. transportation emissions, or 3.3% of total U.S. emissions, and SAF is the only viable option to decarbonize the airline industry in the short term since other low-carbon alternatives, such as hydrogen and battery-powered aircraft technologies, are still early in development. Thus, the aviation industry is mostly reliant on sustainable drop-in fuels from renewable feedstocks. Estimates say these fuels are targeted for a life cycle emission reduction of at least 50% compared to conventional petroleum jet fuel.

Exhibit 2: The increasing domestic production of biofuels

The International Air Transport Association believes that SAF will contribute to around two-thirds of the aviation industry's 2050 net-zero emission efforts. Also, the SAF market is expected to grow rapidly in the coming years, as 38 of the top 50 airlines have committed to achieving net-zero by 2050, with the publicly announced SAF offtake agreements nearly doubling between 2021 and 2022.

Exhibit 3: Net-Zero scenario for the aviation sector

Although the macroeconomic outlook for SAF is promising, investors need to monitor the changes to the renewable energy policy framework if Donald Trump wins the upcoming Presidential election, as he does not seem to be as keen as the Biden administration to support the growth of renewables.

Funding From DOE Will Accelerate Gevo's Commercialization Strategy

The Net-Zero 1 project of Gevo is an initiative aimed at producing SAF and other renewable liquid hydrocarbons, spanning 240 acres in Lake Preston. According to a recent report from Charles River Associates, Net-Zero 1 is projected to generate significant economic and climate benefits. Currently, the domestic SAF production in the U.S. is around 30 million gallons per year. Once the NZ 1 facility reaches full capacity, Gevo's output alone will represent more than double the present annual production. SAF production costs are expected to be competitive with fossil jet fuel, with better CO2 emissions.

Exhibit 4: Cash cost comparison between SAF and Jet Fuel

Additionally, these fully synthetic and interchangeable drop-in fuels of Gevo enable rapid adoption with minimal or no modifications to existing engines or distribution and fueling infrastructure, making it an excellent net-zero substitute for conventional fuels. Gevo's analysis with the 40B SAF-GREET model says this drop-in replacement fuel will reduce lifecycle emissions compared to fossil jet fuel, enabling over 600,000 metric tons of CO2 emissions annually. Gevo has also reached out to many local farmers in the vicinity of the project location who farm approximately 995 fields and 65,800 acres, as corn will be one of the main inputs in Gevo's sustainable aviation fuel production process.

Net-Zero SAF projects are also expected to catalyze the increased adoption of climate-smart agricultural practices. Once completed, the NZ 1 model will serve as a template for future Gevo net-zero projects to accelerate broader SAF commercialization efforts.

Exhibit 5: Gevo's current target markets

For Gevo, the DOE loan commitment substantially reduces the project's execution risk profile. This conditional commitment can be seen as a pivotal step in attracting further capital investments necessary for the commercialization of SAF.

Gevo Is Not Immune To Competitive Threats

Although Gevo is well-positioned to benefit from the expected growth of the SAF market aided by government funding and favorable regulatory decisions, the company is likely to face substantial competitive threats in the future, especially from well-capitalized energy giants looking to make it big in the renewable energy sector.

These energy giants have a few advantages over Gevo, including access to significant financial resources, existing refining infrastructure, and technical expertise. Given the lack of mass production facilities, Gevo cannot capitalize on its first-mover advantages, which leaves room for disruption in the sustainable fuel industry.

In addition to competitive threats, investors may also want to keep an eye on regulatory risks that may arise from a change in the political regime following the upcoming election.

Too Risky To Bet on GEVO Yet

With the U.S. government's and aviation industry's lower carbon emission goals perfectly aligning with one another, SAF demand is likely to see a boost in the coming years. Because of the drop-in nature of these fuels, many airlines are willing to switch to this greener option. Gevo is currently in the process of finalizing engineering designs and securing financing for its Net-Zero 1 facility, which is projected to go live 24-36 months after funding is secured. Gevo's clientele includes many leading names, including Qatar Airways, American Airlines (AAL), and Japan Airlines, and all these major clients are likely to partner with Gevo if the company can offer SAF at a comparable cost to fossil jet fuel. With contracted demand for SAF being around 350 million gallons per year, which is five times Gevo's current production capacity, the newly secured loan facility for SAF production should help the company gain traction by catering to the strong demand for SAF.

All that said, I believe the risks discussed in the previous segment make Gevo a very risky bet on the future of sustainable fuel. Gevo, for now, does not seem to be well-positioned to enjoy long-lasting competitive advantages, which is something I look for when investing in a young company. Gevo may also tap into capital markets sooner rather than later to raise capital, diluting the ownership of existing investors.

Takeaway

Gevo is an interesting company trying to solve the sustainable energy puzzle. There is a long runway for Gevo to grow, but competition is likely to heat up in the coming years, making it difficult for the company to convert first-mover advantages into tangible financial results. This can still be possible, but for now, betting on that probability seems too risky.

data.title: Strong U.S. Growth Still Expected For Next Week's Q3 GDP Report
data.description:
Will the two hurricanes that hit the US Southeast region recently weigh on the upcoming third-quarter GDP data? Probably, although for the moment the impact is expected to be minimal, based on set of nowcasts compiled by CapitalSpectator.com.

The median growth estimate for the upcoming third-quarter GDP report (due on Oct. 30) is a robust +3.0% (seasonally adjusted annual rate). The nowcast matches the strong rise reported for Q2. Fallout from hurricanes Milton and Helene, it appears, will be slight for the national data.

Yet, the economic cost of the storms for the 2024 hurricane season is projected to be among the expensive in history. One estimate is a $50 billion price tag. But so far, it’s hard to discern any conspicous influence for the GDP nowcasts. Quite the opposite, in fact, for the Atlanta Fed’s GDPNow model, which has lifted its Q3 nowcast this month relative to September estimates.

There has been a noticeable runup in US jobless claims recently, which has been attributed in part to the impact from the hurricanes. New filings for weekly unemployment benefits rose sharply to 260,000 for the week through Oct. 5, the highest in three years, but much of the spike reversed in the following week. Analysts will be watching tomorrow’s update for any lingering effects. The consensus forecast sees claims rising 6,000 to 247,000, according to Econoday.com’s consensus point estimate.

The general consensus at the moment is that while stronger headwinds may be brewing for the fourth quarter, the Q3 GDP report will probably reflect an economy that’s humming. That’s the message in yesterday’s revised IMF economic forecast:

“Projected (US) growth for 2024 has been revised upward to 2.8%, which is 0.2 percentage point higher than the July forecast, on account of stronger outturns in consumption and nonresidential investment. The resilience of consumption is largely the result of robust increases in real wages (especially among lower-income households) and wealth effects.”

After all, the fund boasts an 11.39% yield at the current share price, which is substantially more attractive than most of the domestic market indices:

As we can immediately see, the only fund with a higher yield than the Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II is the Advent Convertible & Income Fund. In isolation, this is something that will likely appeal to most income-focused investors. After all, by investing in this fund instead of one of its peers, an investor would receive a considerably larger amount of income from a given investment. That is naturally very appealing. However, as I stated in a previous article:

The fund’s high yield could also be a sign that the market doubts its ability to sustain the payout going forward. The market has a tendency to price a fund’s shares at a level that will give it a competitive yield after it cuts to the level that the market expects it to pay, after all.

Thus, the fact that the Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II has a substantially higher yield than several of the other funds in the market that also invest in convertible securities could suggest that the market doubts that the fund will be able to continue to pay its distribution much longer. This is something that we will be sure to pay close attention to over the remainder of this article since we do not want the fund to have to reduce its distribution and, by extension, reduce our incomes.

As regular readers might remember, I previously discussed the Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II in mid-April of this year. The equity market has generally been very strong since that time, as the S&P 500 Index (SP500) has been driven upward due to a variety of reasons. These reasons include the market’s highly optimistic views about the productivity gains that could accompany the development of artificial intelligence, the Federal Reserve’s loosening of monetary policy, and almost certainly a desire by investors to protect themselves against the loss of purchasing power that will result from a resurgence of inflation. In a recent article, I described how gold prices are telling us that inflation is still very likely to be a problem going forward. Gold was up again today in spite of broader market weakness, so we continue to see that numerous participants in the market are trying to protect themselves against this phenomenon.

The Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II is not an equity fund, strictly speaking, but convertible securities usually do benefit from strength in the equity markets. As such, we might expect the Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II to have delivered a reasonably attractive performance since our previous discussion.

This assumption certainly proves to be correct, as shares of the Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II have risen by 15.75% since the previous article was published:

We can see that the fund very slightly outperformed the S&P 500 Index over the period. This is actually quite surprising since we would not normally expect convertible bonds to keep pace with common stocks. Common stocks are, after all, theoretically riskier instruments because they sit lower in a company’s capital stack and are not required to make regular payments to their owners. In fact, we can see that common stocks did outperform the convertible bond index, so the strong performance appears to be something that is unique to this fund. It is certainly possible that the fund’s leverage boosted the total returns that it earned from its portfolio, but it is equally likely that the fund’s share price delivered a higher return than the portfolio did over the period. This has been the case for many other closed-end funds so far this year, so we will be sure to have a look at that later in today’s article.

Many readers may notice that the Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II did not beat the S&P 500 Index by very much over the period. The difference here was only ten basis points. However, as I stated in the previous article on this fund:

The share price performance of a closed-end fund does not tell the whole story. In fact, investors in these funds tend to do much better than the share price movements would suggest. This is because closed-end funds tend to pay out most or all of their investment profits to the shareholders in the form of distributions. The basic business model is to try and keep the portfolio’s size relatively stable over time while giving the investors all of the profits. This is the reason why closed-end funds tend to have some of the highest yields available in the market. The distributions also provide a real return on investment that is in addition to the share price movement. As a result, investors who hold a fund for any length of time will receive a higher total return than the share price performance suggests.

In order to get a better understanding of how well investors in this fund have done over the past six months, we should include the distributions that it paid in the performance chart above. When we do that, we get this alternative to the chart above:

This chart seems highly likely to attract potential investors. After all, we can clearly see that the Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II managed to outperform both the domestic convertible bond index and even large-cap common stocks by a very comfortable margin. While most people who would consider a fund like this place the highest priority on maximizing their incomes, I highly doubt that anyone would object to beating other asset classes.

It is important to keep in mind that the past performance of a fund is no guarantee of its future performance. The fund’s positioning and expectations of how well its assets will do in the future play the biggest role in this, not its past results. As such, we will be sure to take a close look at this fund’s assets today and see what changes might have occurred in its portfolio over the past six months. The fund has also released an updated financial report since our last discussion, so we will take a look at that. Let us proceed onward and see what revisions, if any, we need to make to our thesis surrounding this fund.

About The Fund

According to the fund’s website, the Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II has the primary objective of providing its investors with a very high level of total return. This objective makes a great deal of sense given the strategy that the fund intends to employ to achieve this objective. The website describes the strategy in pretty good detail:

The Fund seeks total return through a combination of capital appreciation and high current income.

[The Fund] invests in a diversified portfolio of domestic convertible securities and high-yield bonds rated below investment grade.

Under normal circumstances, the Fund will invest at least 80% of its total assets in a diversified portfolio of convertible securities and non-convertible income-producing securities and also seeks to invest at least 50% of its total assets in convertible securities, but determines its allocation based on changes in equity prices, changes in interest rates, and other economic and market factors. For the convertible portion, Voya Investment Management seeks to capture the upside potential of equities with potentially less volatility than a pure stock investment.

This description makes it quite clear that the Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II invests its assets primarily in fixed-income securities, such as convertible securities and junk bonds. Regular readers might remember that I have criticized fixed-income funds in the past for claiming a total return objective. After all, as I pointed out previously:

Bonds by their very nature are income securities, as they do not deliver any net capital gains over their lifetimes. This makes sense, as an investor will purchase a bond at face value and receive face value back when the bond matures. The only investment return for a bond held over its entire lifetime is the coupon payments made to the bond’s owner. Thus, bonds do not deliver capital appreciation over their lifetimes.

The fact that this fund is investing in convertible securities makes the total return objective appropriate though because convertible bonds are capable of delivering net capital gains over time. This comes from the fact that these fixed-income securities can be converted into common stock, and common stock has unlimited potential for capital gains (at least in theory). This can result in someone who purchases a convertible bond realizing substantially higher returns than would ever be realized from an ordinary bond investment. For example, consider the statement that Forbes made about Tesla’s convertibles back in 2021:

March 1, 2021 makes a historic day for Tesla. It’s the day in which holders of Tesla’s convertible bonds issued back in 2014 will convert those bonds into shares, realizing whopping gains of more than 800%.

It is a safe bet that most investors in ordinary fixed-income assets will never realize an 800% gain from one of those assets. The sole exception might be buying bonds from a bankrupt company that ends up fixing its financial problems. For our purposes today, though, the fact that convertible securities can provide capital gains over time whereas bonds will always be issued and redeemed at face value makes the fund’s objective of achieving a high total return (a combination of both current income and capital appreciation) somewhat feasible.

The fund’s website states that the Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II will usually invest at least 50% of its assets in convertible bonds unless it has a good reason not to. The fund’s most recent semi-annual report provides the following asset allocation as of July 31, 2024:

The biggest thing that we see here is that the fund reduced its allocation to convertible bonds fairly substantially over the period, which was offset by increases in both equity-linked notes and cash equivalents in the money market.

Equity-linked notes have a few things in common with convertible bonds. Perhaps most importantly, equity-linked notes do benefit from a rising equity price and thus allow investors in these securities to benefit from the growth and prosperity of the underlying company. While the exact structure of these structured financial assets differs from security to security, it normally has a similar payoff to owning a bond along with a call option on the company’s stock. Convertible bonds also usually benefit from rising equity prices because the conversion feature becomes more valuable to others in the market. Thus, the reduction in convertible bonds and increase in equity-linked notes did not really alter the potential of the portfolio to deliver bond-like yields and common stock appreciation.

It is uncertain why this fund would increase its cash equivalent position to such a large extent. As regular readers know, it is rather unusual to see any closed-end fund have more than 5% of its assets in cash. These funds are usually fully invested, and the fact that they do not have to redeem shares (as open-end funds do) means that they do not have to have much in the way of a cash allocation at any given time. The fund’s semi-annual report does not provide any rationale for this, but it did make one statement that might prove insightful:

The six-month period saw many global equity markets reach new highs before volatility resurfaced in July. Factors including optimism about artificial intelligence and the continued strength of the U.S. economy were favorable for returns. But as inflation slowed and unemployment rose, fears of recession contributed to a downturn in large technology stocks in July. All eyes were on the Federal Reserve, waiting to see when the central bank would begin to cut interest rates.

This statement makes note of the fact that volatility increased in July 2024, particularly surrounding the mega-cap technology stocks that were responsible for a significant proportion of the total returns of the S&P 500 Index year-to-date at that point. Take a look at the performance of the “Magnificent 7,” which are almost certainly the companies that the quote is referring to, over the January 1, 2024, to July 31, 2024, period:

This table summarizes the performance of these companies as the chart cuts off Amazon’s price return:

The S&P 500 Index returned 16.43% over the same period, so it is certainly not an understatement to see that the performance of these seven stocks was responsible for a significant proportion of the market’s overall performance. After all, the S&P 500 Index is market capitalization-weighted, so these huge companies will exert a much greater impact on the index than a smaller company would.

Now, take a look at the performance of these seven companies in July 2024:

In table form:

We can see that five of the “Magnificent 7” stocks handed losses to their investors over the month of July. This is the volatility that the quote from the semi-annual report above makes mention of. It is possible that the fund’s management thought that it would be appropriate to increase the fund’s cash position and reduce its exposure to the markets somewhat because of the uncertainty that was experienced in July. After all, the Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II is a leveraged fund, and leverage can be dangerous during periods of volatility. In such an environment, it makes sense to increase the cash allocation in order to reduce the risk of large losses. This would seem to be the most logical reason for the fund to have such a large cash position in any case.

With that said, we do not see any of the “Magnificent 7” stocks among the largest holdings in the fund. The website provides the following list:

It is unsurprising that the fund would not have significant exposure to the “Magnificent 7” companies as very few convertible bond funds do. Convertible bonds are ordinarily issued by young unprofitable companies or companies that are in some sort of distress and need to entice investors to provide them with financing. With the exception of Tesla ten years ago, that does not really describe the mega-cap technology companies. We do still see that there are a number of other technology companies on the list though. For example, Uber Technologies (UBER), Zillow Group (Z), Coinbase (COIN), and arguably even Affirm Holdings (AFRM) would all likely be classified as technology companies of some form. Thus, we do still see that a sizable proportion of the fund is invested in young companies with limited profitability.

There have been a number of changes to the top ten holdings list since the last time that we discussed this fund. In particular, Palo Alto Networks (PANW), Block (SQ), CCF Holdings (CCF), Akamai Technologies (AKAM), Royal Caribbean (RCL), and Seagate HDD Cayman (STX) were removed from their former positions among the largest positions in the fund. In their places, we have Uber Technologies, PG&E (PCG), Zillow, NextEra Energy Capital Holdings (NEE), Coinbase, and Parsons Corporation (PSN). That is a lot of changes for a nine-month period (the chart in the previous article was dated December 29, 2024), which suggests that the Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II is likely to have a very high turnover rate.

The fund’s semi-annual report confirms this assumption, as it states that the Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II had a 60% portfolio turnover for the six-month period that ended on July 31, 2024. This is not an annualized figure, so if we do annualize it then it works out to a 120% turnover ratio. That is higher than this fund has had in past full-year periods:

FY 2024

FY 2023

Portfolio Turnover

108%

72%

(figures are for the twelve-month period ending on January 31 of the respective year)

Prior to 2023, the fund had a different fiscal year, but for the twelve-month periods ending on February 28 or February 29 of 2021, 2020, and 2019, the fund had portfolio turnover rates of 73%, 35%, and 41% respectively. Thus, it appears almost certain that this fund has been engaging in much more trading activity this year than it usually does. However, there is no guarantee that it will maintain the same pace during the August 2024 to January 2025 period that it did during the most recently completed six-month period.

Leverage

The Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II employs leverage as a method of boosting the effective yield and total return that it earns from the assets in its portfolio. I explained how this works in my previous article on this fund:

Basically, the fund borrows money and then uses that borrowed money to purchase convertible securities, junk bonds, and similar income-producing assets. As long as the total return that the fund receives from the purchased assets is higher than the interest rate that it needs to pay on the borrowed money, the strategy works pretty well to boost the effective yield of the portfolio. This fund is capable of borrowing money at institutional rates, which are considerably lower than retail rates. As such, this will normally be the case.

However, the use of debt in this fashion is a double-edged sword. This is because leverage boosts both gains and losses. As such, we want to ensure that the fund is not employing too much leverage because that would expose us to an excessive amount of risk. I generally do not like a fund’s leverage to exceed a third as a percentage of its assets for that reason.

According to CEF Data, the Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II currently has leveraged assets comprising 37.80% of its overall portfolio. This is a decrease from the 38.54% leverage ratio that the fund had the last time that we discussed it. It is understandable that the fund’s leverage would decline over the period as its share price has risen by 15.75%. All else being equal, a rising share price should correspond to a growing portfolio and thus the fund’s outstanding borrowings as a percentage of the portfolio would be reduced. However, a 15.75% increase in the size of the fund’s portfolio should have decreased its leverage by a lot more than 74 basis points.

This situation is easily resolved when we realize that the fund’s portfolio did not gain 15.75% over the period. This chart shows the fund’s net asset value from April 14, 2024 (the date that my previous article was published) through today:

As we can clearly see, the fund’s net asset value is only up by 5.26% over the period. Thus, the fund’s shares substantially outperformed the underlying assets over the period, and so the fund is going to be more expensive today than it was previously. We will discuss this later in this article. Overall, we still see that a rising portfolio did reduce the fund’s leverage, but it was not nearly as strong as a performance as we would expect.

Unfortunately, though, we do still see that the leverage of the Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II is well above the one-third of assets level that we would ordinarily consider to be acceptable from a risk-management perspective. As we can see here, it is also more leveraged than its peers:

Fund Name

Leverage Ratio

Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II

37.80%

Advent Convertible & Income Fund

41.73%

Bancroft Fund

21.00%

Calamos Convertible Opportunities & Income Fund

37.28%

Ellsworth Growth and Income Fund

21.00%

High Income Securities Fund

0.00%

(all figures from CEF Data)

As we can clearly see, there is only one fund in this group that employs a higher level of leverage than the Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II. This might be a sign that this fund is employing more leverage than is really safe given its strategy. That could prove concerning to risk-averse investors, as a higher level of leverage typically makes a fund’s price and portfolio performance more volatile. For a retiree or someone else who depends on their portfolio to support their lifestyle, it can be difficult to sleep at night if their portfolio is engaging in wild swings. Anyone who is considering this fund should take their risk tolerance into account before making a purchase.

Distribution Analysis

The primary objective of the Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II is to provide its investors with a high level of total return. As I mentioned in the introduction, closed-end funds tend to primarily deliver their total return through the payment of distributions to their shareholders and this fund is no exception to that. The Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II pays a monthly distribution of $0.03 per share, which gives it an impressive 11.39% yield at the current share price. As we saw in the introduction, this yield compares pretty well with the fund’s peers.

Unfortunately, this fund has not been especially reliable with respect to its distribution over the years:

As I stated in the previous article:

This is almost certainly going to be a turn-off for any investor who is seeking to earn a consistent and secure income from the assets in their portfolios. The fact that this fund has been steadily reducing its distributions is especially problematic considering the very high level of inflation that has been plaguing the economy. After all, the cost of everything that we purchase in our daily lives has been rapidly increasing over the past few years. As such, we need more income today to maintain a given lifestyle than we did only a few years ago. The fact that this fund’s distribution has been steadily declining has had the opposite effect.

As of the time of writing, the most recent financial report that is available for the Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II is the semi-annual report for the six-month period that ended on July 31, 2024. This is a newer report than the one that we had available to us the last time that we discussed this fund, so let us have a look at it and see if the fund has managed to fix its previous problem of insufficient distribution coverage.

For the six-month period that ended on July 31, 2024, the Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II received $8.051 million in interest and $832,000 in dividends from the assets in its portfolio. When we combine this with a small amount of income from other sources, we see that the fund had a total investment income of $8.930 million for the period. The fund paid its expenses out of this amount, which left it with $5.888 million available for shareholders. This was insufficient to cover the $13.701 million that the fund paid out in distributions over the period.

Fortunately, the fund was able to make up the difference through capital gains. For the six-month period that ended on July 31, 2024, the Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II reported net realized gains of $3.650 million along with $9.889 million in net unrealized gains. Overall, the fund’s net assets increased by $2.745 million after accounting for all inflows and outflows during the period.

We can immediately see that the fund was able to cover its distribution during the six-month period, which was a nice change from its failure to cover during the previous full-year period. However, this fund only managed to cover its payout because of its sizable level of unrealized gains. These can be erased if the market corrects, so there is no guarantee that the distributions will not ultimately prove destructive to net asset value. However, there is no foreseeable event in the near future that could cause such a correction so for now, investors should probably not need to worry about the fund’s distribution.

Valuation

Shares of the Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II are currently trading at a 7.35% discount on net asset value. This is a less attractive price than the 8.53% discount that the shares have had on average over the past month.

Expenses

The Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II has an expense ratio of 4.81%, which will undoubtedly seem very high when compared to most exchange-traded funds and other passively managed investments. However, the majority of the fund’s expenses are interest costs related to its leverage. If we exclude these costs, the fund’s expense ratio comes in at 1.28%. That is still expensive, but not ludicrously so when compared to other actively managed funds. For example, here is how this fund compares with its peers:

Fund Name

Expense Ratio

Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II

Calamos Convertible Opportunities & Income Fund

(all figures from CEF Data)

We can immediately see that the Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II does not appear to be that expensive if we exclude the interest on its leverage. The big thing that makes this fund pricy is the high level of leverage that it uses to boost its returns. If properly executed, the leverage should boost the fund’s returns by more than it costs to carry it. This will not always be the case though but considering that the Federal Reserve is lowering interest rates, and the market is exhibiting strength, it should be okay for now.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the Virtus Convertible & Income Fund II appears to have improved on the problems that it had the last time that we discussed it. In particular, it did manage to cover the distributions that it paid out during the first half of the current fiscal year. The fund’s increased cash position is a bit confusing, but this might be a temporary measure that it implemented to reduce risk in the face of July’s volatility. It is possible that the fund has since deployed it. The fund’s portfolio performance has not been nearly as good as its recent share price performance suggests, but it is not expensive yet.
